what: Psychometric Analysis in R with Rasch Measurement Theory
title: Psychometric Analysis in R with Rasch Measurement Theory
text: R package Rasch Measurement Theory psychometrics, intended use Quarto documentation presentation analysis process results. package largely functions wrapper R packages Rasch analyses, primarily eRm, mirt, psychotree, iarm, catR, TAM. use easyRasch publication, please also cite packages. can find citation information R using citation('packagename'). package intended simplify Rasch analysis process provides easy creation tables figures functions options. package tested MacOS Windows R 4.1 4.4. also created package Bayesian Rasch models, based brms package. can find easyRaschBayes CRAN. NOTE: package formerly known RISEkbmRasch. old GitHub page code commit history RISEkbmRasch remain available public archive read-state. Please regularly check Changelog notes updates. vignette recommended reading skimmed README. find sample Rasch analysis vignette, output package functions. vignette produced using Quarto, source code course also available. functions developed analysis polytomous data (two response categories), using Rasch partial credit model (PCM). Also, choice made rely primarily conditional maximum likelihood (CML) estimation item parameters, since robust various conditions enables “person-free assessment”.

title: Psychometric Analysis in R with Rasch Measurement Theory
text: Load package: functions package relatively simple wrappers create outputs tables figures make Rasch analysis process quick visual. primary introduction using package vignette. two data structure requirements: need create dataframe object named itemlabels consists two variables/columns: first variable named itemnr, containing variable names exactly named dataframe containing data (example q1, q2, q3, etc) second variable named item, containing either questionnaire item description (description task, etc) Sample code four items: response data want analyze needs dataframe participants rows items columns/variables, response data dataframe. lowest response category needs zero (0) items. See https://pgmj.github.io/datawrangling.html#recoding-response-categories sample R code recoding. need separate demographic variables separate dataframe separate vectors (preferrably labeled factors), analysis differential item functioning (DIF). remove DIF-variables dataframe item data. dataframe item data can contain item data analysis functions work (ID variable demographic variables). Rasch functions, auto-detection dichotomous polytomous data, Rasch-analysis functions package, separate functions polytomous data (two response options item) dichotomous/binary data. Rating Scale Model (RSM) polytomous data implemented functions. functions use PCM default can use option model = "RM" dichotomous data: RItargeting() RItif() RIloadLoc() RIpfit()

title: Reliability metrics — RIreliability
text: RMU, Relative Measurement Uncertainty: function uses mirt library estimate Rasch model using Marginal Maximum Likelihood generates plausible values (PVs; Mislevy, 1991). function uses borrowed code, see ?RMUreliability. PVs used RMU method described Bignardi et al. (2025) estimate mean confidence interval. mean similar expected posteriori (EAP) reliability point estimate (Adams, 2005). confidence interval uses 95% highest continuous density interval (HDCI) based distribution correlations. Default setting generate 1000 PVs. recommended stable estimates/CIs. many systematically evaluated, 4000 might good starting point. smaller samples, PVs demanding computationally, wary time takes create thousands PVs respondent large samples. PSI, Person Separation Index: Estimated using functions eRm package, see ?eRm::SepRel. Note excludes min/max scoring individuals, may result unexpected results, especially compared methods. Empirical: Estimated using mirt::empirical_rxx(), see https://stats.stackexchange.com/questions/427631/difference--empirical--marginal-reliability---irt-model

title: Reliability metrics — RIreliability
text: Bignardi, G., Kievit, R., & Bürkner, P. C. (2025). general method estimating reliability using Bayesian Measurement Uncertainty. PsyArXiv. doi:10.31234/osf.io/h54k8 Mislevy, R. J. (1991). Randomization-Based Inference Latent Variables Complex Samples. Psychometrika, 56(2), 177–196. doi:10.1007/BF02294457 Adams, R. J. (2005). Reliability measurement design effect. Studies Educational Evaluation, 31(2), 162–172. doi:10.1016/j.stueduc.2005.05.008

title: Reliability metrics — RIreliability
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # comparison of a fully Bayesian Rasch model and PVs df <- eRm::raschdat1[,1:20] %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item")  library(brms) brms_model <- brm(   value ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data    = df,   chains  = 4,   cores   = 4,   family = "bernoulli" )  posterior_draws <- brms_model %>%   as_draws_df() %>%   dplyr::select(starts_with("r_id")) %>%   t()  RMUreliability(posterior_draws) RIreliability(eRm::raschdat1[,1:20], draws = 4000) } # }

Test information shows reliability curve test (sample). Use option samplePSI = TRUE add graphical written representation current sample's theta mean/SD, test information (TIF) mean/SD, Person Separation Index (PSI). According Wright & Stone (1999), PSI calculated \(\frac{\mathrm{SSD}-\mathrm{MSE}}{\mathrm{SSD}}\), see ?eRm::SepRel details. According Embretson & Reise (2000), PSI = 1 - SEM^2, TIF = 1/SEM^2, values reported function based sample average SEM. reference: TIF 2.5 corresponds PSI 0.6 TIF 3.33 -> PSI 0.7 TIF 5 -> PSI 0.8 TIF 10 -> PSI 0.9

title: Estimate reliability (Relative Measurement Uncertainty) from Bayesian measurement models — RMUreliability
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # See https://www.bignardi.co.uk/8_bayes_reliability/tutorial_rmu_sum_score_reliability.html for more details on this example  # Simulate data  set.seed(1) N                   = 5000 # number of subjects (mice) J                   = 3    # number of measurements per subject true_score_variance = 1 error_variance      = 10  df = expand.grid(j = 1:J, mouse = 1:N)  true_scores       = rnorm(N, mean = 10, sd = sqrt(true_score_variance)) measurement_error = rnorm(N*J, mean = 0, sd = sqrt(error_variance))  df$measurement = true_scores[df$mouse] + measurement_error  df_average_lengths = df %>%   group_by(mouse) %>%   summarise(average_measurement = mean(measurement))  # Reliability should equal this:  true_score_variance/(true_score_variance+error_variance/J)  # Approximately the same as:  cor(df_average_lengths$average_measurement, true_scores)^2  # Fit model and calculate RMU  brms_model = brm(   measurement ~ 1 + (1 | mouse),   data    = df )  # Extract posterior draws from brms model  posterior_draws = brms_model %>%   as_draws_df() %>%   select(starts_with("r_mouse")) %>%   t()  # Calculate RMU  reliability(posterior_draws)$hdci } # }

title: Posterior Predictive Item Fit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — fit_statistic_pcm
text: function implements posterior predictive checking approach item fit described Bürkner (2020). procedure works follows: Draw posterior expected category probabilities via posterior_epred posterior predicted responses via posterior_predict. ordinal categorical models (3D array output posterior_epred), extract probability assigned observed response category replicated response category draw observation. Apply user-supplied criterion function compute pointwise fit values observed replicated data. Aggregate (sum) criterion values within level group posterior draw. common choice ordinal IRT models categorical log-likelihood criterion function(y, p) log(p). binary (e.g., dichotomous Rasch) models, Bernoulli log-likelihood function(y, p) y * log(p) + (1 - y) * log(1 - p) may used instead.

title: Posterior Predictive Item Fit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — fit_statistic_pcm
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Polytomous Rasch (Partial Credit Model) ---  # Prepare data in long format df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  # Fit a Partial Credit Model using the adjacent category family fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data    = df_pcm,   family  = acat,   chains  = 4,   cores   = 4,   iter    = 2000 )  # Categorical log-likelihood criterion (for polytomous models) ll_categorical <- function(y, p) log(p)  # Compute item fit statistics item_fit <- fit_statistic_pcm(   model      = fit_pcm,   criterion  = ll_categorical,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 500 )  # Summarise: posterior predictive p-values per item item_fit %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   )  # Use ggplot2 to make a histogram library(ggplot2) item_fit %>%   ggplot(aes(crit_diff)) +   geom_histogram(aes(fill = ifelse(crit_diff > 0, "above","below"))) +   facet_wrap("item") +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "none")  # Compute person fit statistics person_fit <- fit_statistic_pcm(   model      = fit_pcm,   criterion  = ll_categorical,   group      = id,   ndraws_use = 500 ) } # }

title: Posterior Predictive Item Fit Statistic for Binary Bayesian IRT Models — fit_statistic_rm
text: function binary-response counterpart fit_statistic, handles polytomous (ordinal / categorical) models. dichotomous models, posterior_epred() returns 2D matrix (S x N) success probabilities, criterion function receives observed binary response corresponding probability directly. procedure follows posterior predictive checking approach described Bürkner (2020): Draw posterior expected success probabilities via posterior_epred posterior predicted binary responses via posterior_predict. Apply user-supplied criterion function pointwise observed replicated data paired predicted probabilities. Aggregate (sum) criterion values within level group posterior draw. standard criterion binary models Bernoulli log-likelihood: $$\ell(y, p) = y \log(p) + (1 - y) \log(1 - p).$$

title: Posterior Predictive Item Fit Statistic for Binary Bayesian IRT Models — fit_statistic_rm
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Dichotomous Rasch Model ---  # Prepare binary response data in long format df_rm <- eRm::raschdat3 %>%   as.data.frame() %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  # Fit a dichotomous Rasch model fit_rm <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  # Bernoulli log-likelihood criterion ll_bernoulli <- function(y, p) y * log(p) + (1 - y) * log(1 - p)  # Compute item fit statistics item_fit <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_rm,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 500 )  # Summarise: posterior predictive p-values per item item_fit %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   )  # Use ggplot2 to make a histogram library(ggplot2) item_fit %>%   ggplot(aes(crit_diff)) +   geom_histogram(aes(fill = ifelse(crit_diff > 0, "above","below"))) +   facet_wrap("item") +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "none")  # Compute person fit statistics person_fit <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_rm,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  person_fit %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   )  # --- 1PL model with item-specific intercepts ---  # Alternative parameterisation with fixed item effects fit_1pl <- brm(   response ~ 0 + item + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  item_fit_1pl <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_1pl,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 500 )  item_fit_1pl %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   ) } # }

title: Posterior Predictive Infit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — infit_statistic
text: Computes Bayesian analogue conditional item infit statistic (described Christensen, Kreiner & Mesbah, 2013) Rasch-family models fitted brms. posterior draw, expected values variances derived category probabilities returned posterior_epred, variance-weighted standardised residuals computed observed replicated data. result can summarised posterior predictive p-values assess item fit.

title: Posterior Predictive Infit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — infit_statistic
text: tibble following columns: item item identifier. draw Integer index posterior draw. infit observed infit statistic item draw. infit_rep replicated infit statistic (based posterior predicted data) item draw. outfit observed outfit statistic item draw. outfit_rep replicated outfit statistic item draw. output grouped item variable. Posterior predictive p-values can obtained computing, e.g., mean(infit_rep > infit) within item.

title: Posterior Predictive Infit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — infit_statistic
text: procedure adapts conditional infit/outfit statistics (Christensen et al., 2013; Kreiner & Christensen, 2011; Müller, 2020) Bayesian framework: posterior draw \(s\), category probabilities \(P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)\) obtained posterior_epred. conditional expected value variance observation computed : $$E^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c c \cdot P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)$$ $$Var^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c (c - E^{(s)}_{vi})^2 \cdot       P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)$$ Standardised squared residuals : $$Z^{2(s)}_{vi} = (X_{vi} - E^{(s)}_{vi})^2 / Var^{(s)}_{vi}$$ Outfit unweighted mean \(Z^{2}_{vi}\) across persons within item. Infit variance-weighted mean: $$Infit^{(s)}_i = \frac{\sum_v Var^{(s)}_{vi} \cdot       Z^{2(s)}_{vi}}{\sum_v Var^{(s)}_{vi}}$$ computations repeated replicated data \(Y^{rep}\) drawn via posterior_predict. perfect fit, infit outfit expected value 1. Values substantially 1 indicate underfit (much noise), values 1 indicate overfit (little variation, e.g., redundancy). Posterior predictive p-values near 0 1 indicate misfit.

Bayesian Item Response Modeling R brms Stan. Journal Statistical Software, 100, 1–54. doi:10.18637/jss.v100.i05 Christensen, K. B., Kreiner, S. & Mesbah, M. (Eds.) (2013). Rasch Models Health. Item fit statistics Rasch analysis: can trust ? Journal Statistical Distributions Applications, 7(1). doi:10.1186/s40488-020-00108-7

title: Posterior Predictive Infit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — infit_statistic
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Partial Credit Model (polytomous) ---  df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data   = df_pcm,   family = acat,   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  # Compute infit per item item_infit <- infit_statistic(   model      = fit_pcm,   item_var   = item,   person_var = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  # Summarise across draws item_infit %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     infit_obs = mean(infit),     infit_rep = mean(infit_rep),     infit_ppp = mean(infit_rep > infit)   )  # --- Dichotomous Rasch Model ---  df_rm <- eRm::raschdat3 %>%   as.data.frame() %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_rm <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  item_infit_rm <- infit_statistic(   model      = fit_rm,   item_var   = item,   person_var = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  item_infit_rm %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     infit_obs = mean(infit),     infit_rep = mean(infit_rep),     infit_ppp = mean(infit_rep > infit)   ) } # }

title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
text: Computes Bayesian analogue Yen's Q3 statistic (Yen, 1984) detecting local dependence item pairs Rasch-family models fitted brms. posterior draw, residual correlations computed observed replicated data, yielding posterior predictive p-value item pair automatically calibrated without requiring knowledge sampling distribution.

title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
text: model fitted brmsfit object ordinal IRT model (e.g., family = acat partial credit model) dichotomous model (family = bernoulli()). item_var unquoted variable name identifying item grouping variable model data (e.g., item). person_var unquoted variable name identifying person grouping variable model data (e.g., id). ndraws_use Optional positive integer. specified, random subset posterior draws size used. NULL (default), draws used.

title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
text: tibble following columns: item_1 First item pair. item_2 Second item pair. q3_obs Posterior mean observed Q3 residual correlation. q3_rep Posterior mean replicated Q3 residual correlation. q3_diff Posterior mean q3_obs - q3_rep. Large positive values indicate observed residual correlation exceeds model expects. ppp Posterior predictive p-value: mean(q3_obs > q3_rep) across draws. Values close 1 indicate local dependence (observed correlation systematically higher replicated). q3_obs_q025, q3_obs_q975 2.5\ (95\ observed Q3. q3_obs_q005, q3_obs_q995 0.5\ (99\ observed Q3. q3_diff_q025, q3_diff_q975 2.5\ (95\ Q3 differences. q3_diff_q005, q3_diff_q995 0.5\ (99\ Q3 differences.

title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
text: procedure works follows posterior draw \(s\): Compute expected values \(E^{(s)}_{vi}\) category probabilities returned posterior_epred. ordinal models: \(E^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c c \cdot     P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)\). binary models: \(E^{(s)}_{vi} =     P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = 1)\). Compute observed residuals: \(d^{(s)}_{vi} = X_{vi} -     E^{(s)}_{vi}\). Compute replicated residuals: \(d^{rep(s)}_{vi} =     Y^{rep(s)}_{vi} - E^{(s)}_{vi}\), \(Y^{rep}\) drawn via posterior_predict. item pair \((, j)\), compute Q3 Pearson correlation residuals across persons responded items. Aggregate across draws obtain posterior means, quantiles, posterior predictive p-values. key advantage parametric bootstrapping reference distribution obtained directly posterior, automatically accounting parameter uncertainty negative bias inherent Q3 (depends test length person ability distribution).

title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
text: Yen, W. M. (1984). Effects local item dependence fit equating performance three-parameter logistic model. Applied Psychological Measurement, 8(2), 125–145. Christensen, K. B., Makransky, G. & Horton, M. (2017). Critical values Yen's Q3: Identification local dependence Rasch model using residual correlations. Applied Psychological Measurement, 41(3), 178–194. doi:10.1177/0146621616677520 Bürkner, P.-C. (2020). Analysing Standard Progressive Matrices (SPM-LS) Bayesian Item Response Models. Journal Intelligence, 8(1). doi:10.3390/jintelligence8010005 Bürkner, P.-C. (2021). Bayesian Item Response Modeling R brms Stan. Journal Statistical Software, 100, 1–54. doi:10.18637/jss.v100.i05

title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Partial Credit Model ---  df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data   = df_pcm,   family = acat,   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  # Q3 residual correlations q3_results <- q3_statistic(   model      = fit_pcm,   item_var   = item,   person_var = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  # Flag item pairs with ppp > 0.95 as locally dependent q3_results %>%   filter(ppp > 0.95) %>%   arrange(desc(q3_diff))  # Inspect 99% credible intervals for Q3 differences q3_results %>%   filter(q3_diff_q005 > 0)  # --- Dichotomous Rasch Model ---  df_rm <- eRm::raschdat3 %>%   as.data.frame() %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_rm <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  q3_rm <- q3_statistic(   model      = fit_rm,   item_var   = item,   person_var = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  q3_rm %>%   filter(ppp > 0.95) } # }
